intro:
A door or gate is a point of passage — the controlled opening through which one enters or is shut out. In the biblical world the gate was far more than an architectural detail: the city gate was the place of justice, commerce, and public life, where elders sat to judge and decisions were made, while the door of a house marked the boundary between the safety of the family and the danger outside. Out of this everyday reality Scripture builds a rich theology of access: who may enter, and how.

The motif gathers weight around the presence of God. The blood of the Passover lamb is daubed on the doorframe so that judgment passes over the household within (Exodus 12:7). The tabernacle and temple are entered through a series of guarded openings, teaching that approach to the Holy One is real but regulated. Jacob, waking from his dream at Bethel, cries, "This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven" (Genesis 28:17). The Psalms turn the image into worship: "Open to me the gates of righteousness... This is the gate of the LORD; the righteous shall enter through it" (Psalm 118:19–20), and the great processional summons, "Lift up your heads, O gates... that the King of glory may come in" (Psalm 24:7).

In the Gospels Jesus claims the image as a title for himself. "I am the door," he says; "if anyone enters by me, he will be saved and will go in and out and find pasture" (John 10:9). He is the one entrance to the safety of the flock and to the Father, and he warns that the way to life is a narrow gate that few find (Matthew 7:13–14). He also stands as the seeker at the threshold of the human heart: "Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in" (Revelation 3:20). To his church he sets before it "an open door, which no one is able to shut" (Revelation 3:8).

The story ends with gates that no longer exclude. In the new Jerusalem the gates "will never be shut by day — and there will be no night there" (Revelation 21:25), for the access once barred at Eden's entrance is opened forever to all who come through Christ the door. The motif thus presses the question every reader must answer: have I entered by the one gate that leads to life?
